coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by carbon quantum dots-ZnFe2O4 composites
coredata.dc:creator: Zaman, Fakhr uz
Kumar, Anuj
Yasin, Ghulam
Boakye, Felix Ofori
Muhammad, Fawad
Iqbal, Sikandar
Alotaibi, Khalid M.
Hou, Linrui
Yuan, Changzhou
coredata.dc:description: To safely cope with the hazardous methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine (RhB) dyes in wastewater, ZnFe2O4 has shown promise as attractive photocatalytic degradation material. Therefore, we synthesized CQDs-decorated ZnFe2O4 microspheres and successfully degraded MB and RhB dyes under UV light. The CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites exhibited superior performance as compared to pure ZnFe2O4, achieving nearly 100% degradation of MB in 60 min and 95% degradation of RhB in 80 min. Additionally, the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 material was found stable after four consecutive cycles during the photocatalytic activity. Density functional theory calculations revealed that CQDs generated new gap states near the Fermi level, enhancing the charge separation and conductivity of the composites. The photocatalytic mechanism involved the generation of holes, . OH and . O2 radicals as the active species. These results demonstrate that CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites can be promising candidates in wastewater treatment along with other wide range of applications.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by carbon quantum dots-ZnFe2O4 composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To safely cope with the hazardous methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine (RhB) dyes in wastewater, ZnFe2O4 has shown promise as attractive photocatalytic degradation material. Therefore, we synthesized CQDs-decorated ZnFe2O4 microspheres and successfully degraded MB and RhB dyes under UV light. The CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites exhibited superior performance as compared to pure ZnFe2O4, achieving nearly 100% degradation of MB in 60 min and 95% degradation of RhB in 80 min. Additionally, the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 material was found stable after four consecutive cycles during the photocatalytic activity. Density functional theory calculations revealed that CQDs generated new gap states near the Fermi level, enhancing the charge separation and conductivity of the composites. The photocatalytic mechanism involved the generation of holes, . OH and . O2 radicals as the active species. These results demonstrate that CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites can be promising candidates in wastewater treatment along with other wide range of applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-increasing scalable utilization of organic dyes in the textile industry and their byproducts have raised severe concerns about aquatic and non-aquatic life [1,2] . Although many attempts are underway to cope with this problem, according to environmental scientists, the threat can be mitigated through a safe photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by utilizing easily available sunlight or ultraviolet radiation [3–5] . However, to date, photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes still lacks high efficiency [6–9] . Semiconducting metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, and WO3) are effective photocatalysts for eliminating organic and inorganic contaminants when exposed to visible or solar light due to their bandgap energy, high light absorption, and appropriate physicochemical and catalytic characteristics [10,11]. Chemically, spinel ferrite nanoparticles provide an attractive surface for tuning the interfacial characteristics and exhibit excellent blend stability. Innovative magnetic-based semiconductor photocatalysts are critical for overcoming separating and secondary pollution issues to exploit spinel ferrites fully. Among them, ZnFe2O4 is an inorganic semiconductor with a comparatively narrow bandgap of 2.2–1.7 eV, which is extensively investigated in various applications, such as solar transformation, photocatalysis, and photochemical hydrogen production from water. This is due to its advantages of visible-light response, ease of synthesis, low cost, and high photochemical stability [12]. Moreover, the attractive characteristics of ferrites depend on the dispersion of cations between octahedral and tetrahedral locations within the crystal structures, controlling cation transport provides a means of tailoring their features. Cation transport depends on the ionic electrical configuration, valence, and the size of the nanoparticles. Unfortunately, the low visible light sensitivity and recycling difficulty of bare ZnFe2O4 photocatalysts preclude their practical applications [13,14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a unique spherical carbon material with a diameter of less than 10 nm, have garnered considerable interest in recent years owing to their low cost, nontoxicity, strong thermal and chemical stability, and ease of synthesis [15–20]. CQDs have superior electron transfer/reservoir properties due to their conjugated structure [21–23]. Additionally, the up-converted PL feature of CQDs enables the absorption zone of wide-bandgap semiconductors to be extended to the visible or near-infrared [24–26]. As a result, CQDs are used in constructing nanocomposites with other semiconductors to efficiently separate photo-excited electron-hole pairs and efficiently exploit the entire spectrum of sunlight, both of which are critical for photocatalytic activity. CQDs have been effectively integrated into semiconductors, including metallic oxides [27–31], polymers (g-C3N4) [32,33], ternary sulfides, etc. [34,35]. Interestingly, sizable materials could not produce a flawless interface due to interfacial imperfections forming recombination centers for photo-excited electron-hole pairs [30,36]. However, the tiny size and abundance of surface functional groups of CQDs can help minimize the interfacial misfit between them and other semiconductors, resulting in a close interface that is advantageous for charge transfer and increasing photocatalytic activity [37–40].

As a result, combining CQDs and ZnFe2O4 can enhance the overall photodegradation efficiency of organic dyes (MB and RhB) compared to individual catalysts. Due to their high surface area, CQDs can provide more dye adsorption sites, increasing their photodegradation rate. The novelty lies in using CQDs as a photosensitizer in combination with ZnFe2O4, which has not been previously explored for the photodegradation of MB and RhB dyes. This combined catalyst system may improve performance in higher photodegradation rates and lower catalyst dosage requirements. The present work describes the synthesis of unique CQDs-modified ZnFe2O4 composites. In-depth examinations of the morphologies, structures, optical characteristics, and photocatalytic properties are conducted. The photocatalytic degradation of MB and RhB organic dyes determines the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 samples. Due to the synergistic effect of light harvesting and increased separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, CQDs/ZnFe2O4 samples exhibit higher photocatalytic activity.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2•6 H2O), iron nitrate hexahydrate (Fe(NO3)2•6 H2O), isopropanol, 1,2 propanediol, Methylene blue (MB), Rhodamine B (RhB), and ethanol (95%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent limited company [M1], and used as received and without any further purification.

Carbon quantum dots were effectively synthesized using a previously described approach that used the citric acid breakdown technique to produce water-soluble CQDs[41]. A typical reaction involved heating 10 g of citric acid for 40 hours at 180°C in an electric oven. The gelatinous dark residue was entirely dissolved in a 5 M NaOH solution with a pH of 7. Lastly, we used freeze-drying to obtain CQDs powder.

ZnFe2O4 MSs were prepared through a microwave-assisted method. In a typical process, 1 mmol of (Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O) and 2 mmol of (Fe(NO3)2.6 H2O) were completely dissolved in a mixture of 30 mL of isopropanol and 10 mL of 1,2 propanediol solvent to get a clear solution. Then the resultant solution was irritated in a microwave reactor for 5 min at 120 ºC. After cooling, the sample was thoroughly washed by ethanol, dried in an oven at 60 ºC for 24 h, and then heated in a muffle furnace at 500 ºC for 5 hours. Finally, ZnFe2O4 MSs were obtained.

Specifically, 0.15, 0.30, and 0.45 g of CQDs powders were placed into three separate beakers, and the synthesized samples were named 0.15 CQDs, 0.30 CQDs, and 0.45 CQDs, respectively. Each beaker was then filled with 0.1 g ZnFe2O4 powders and 15 mL ethanol solution. After five hours of ultrasonically mixing the three liquid suspensions, they were transferred to three porcelain boats and dried in a fuming cupboard. The porcelain boats were then moved to a muffle furnace and calcined for 4 h at 300 °C. The collected gray-black particles were washed with water and ethanol and then dried in an oven overnight.

XRD was used to study the crystal structure and phase purity of ZnFe2O4 and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 with varying quantities of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 b the diffraction peaks observed in all samples are indicative of the presence of ZnFe2O4, which possesses a cubic spinel structure. The peaks observed at 2θ values of 29.91°, 35.26°, 42.84°, 56.62°, and 62.21° in the X-ray diffraction pattern of ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS No. 22–1012) can be attributed to the reflections corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) crystallographic planes, respectively. [42]. Although no typical CQDs peak (approximately 26°) was observed in the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs, this could be ascribed to the low concentration and high distribution of CQDs in the synthesized materials [21]. Furthermore, the average crystallite sizes are calculated according to the Debye– Scherrer formula as follows:
D = Kλ/β cos θ
.

where D is the crystallite size, K is the shape constant (K = 0.9), λ is the wavelength of X-ray (λ = 0.154 nm), β is the full-width half maximum (FWHM) and θ is the incident angle of X-ray. The diffraction peak position, full half-width maximum, and crystallite sizes of all samples are calculated and shown in (Table S1). Based on these calculations the crystal structure of ZnFe2O4 for all samples remains unchanged only the intensity of all the peaks is decreased because of the addition of the CQDs as they are amorphous in nature and it will not change the crystal structure of ZnFe2O4 [43].

Additionally, CQD content on ZnFe2O4 was determined using thermogravimetric (TG) analysis, as shown in Fig. 1 c. 1.52% of the weight loss in pure ZnFe2O4 was attributed to the elimination of adsorbed water molecules at temperatures below 200 °C[44]. By varying the weight percent of CQDs in this region, the associated weight loss increased, indicating that CQDs promoted the sorption of water molecules. Beyond this range, weight loss was ascribed to surface-adsorbed moisture, residual organics, OH species, and CQD combustion. A significant weight loss was observed for the CQD-coated ZnFe2O4, suggesting that the presence of CQDs on ZnFe2O4 resulted in the release of more combustion products. The consistent weight losses for pure ZnFe2O4 and 0.15, 0.30, and 0.45 wt% CQDs decorated were calculated as 0.62%, 8.85% and 5.35% respectively. Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) and Raman spectra are collected to further support the presence of carbon quantum dots in the resulting CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. Fig. 1d and Table S2 represent the FT-IR spectrum and functional group of ZnFe2O4 and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs performed at room temperature within the range of 4000–400 cm-1. At 2383 cm−1, the observed broadband is due to the O–H stretching vibrations of adsorbed water molecules [45,46]. The peaks at 438 and 553 cm−1 correspond to the intrinsic lattice vibrations of Fe–O and Zn–O, respectively, which are octahedral and tetrahedral coordination compounds found in the spinel structure of ZnFe2O4 [45,47]. Moreover, some new organic functional groups have appeared after adding CQDs. The stretching vibrations of CO and COOH can be held responsible for the newly developed absorption bands at 1645 cm-1 and 1406 cm-1, correspondingly [48,49].

Fig. 1 e shows the Raman spectroscopy measurements for the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs in the 200–1800 cm−1 range at ambient temperature for the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. The typical spinel structure (AB2O4) exhibited five prominent Raman peaks at 293, 351, 506, 651, and 691 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrational modes (A1 g + Eg +3 T2 g) in ZnFe2O4 cubic spinel. The low-frequency mode at 233 cm−1, which corresponds to the properties of octahedral sites (BO6), is ascribed to Eg symmetry, whereas the ones at 351 and 476 cm−1 are attributed to T2g symmetry. The A1 g symmetry, which incorporates the vibrations of the oxygen atom in AO4 tetrahedral groups, is related to the high-frequency modes at 651 and 691 cm−1. Thus, the frequency mode detected at 651 cm−1 is due to oxygen vibrations against zinc, while the mode observed at 691 cm−1 is due to oxygen vibrations against iron [50]. Additionally, the two bands at 1094 and 1194 likely represent overtone peaks, as previously described in the literature [51]. Moreover, two peaks positioned at 1315 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 are due to the G and D bands of carbons, correspondingly consistent with the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons in the sample [52]. The chemical composition and valence states of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs were investigated further using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The characteristic survey spectra of the CDQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs are displayed in Fig. 1 f (Fig. S1), which demonstrates the existence of the elements Zn, Fe, O, and C. Fig. 1g (Fig S2a and S3a) depicts the Fe2p core level spectrum deconvolution, which has two typical peaks for Fe3+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 with binding energies of 714.3 and 728.9 eV, correspondingly. At 711.07 and 720.09 eV, the Fe3+ satellite peaks were discovered. Additionally, the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 synthesized samples exhibited binding energies of 711 and 724.8 eV for Fe2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, accordingly indicating the presence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation states[53]. As illustrated in Fig. 1 h (Fig. S2b, S3b) the characteristic peaks of Zn2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 were detected at 1021.2 and 1044.8 eV, respectively. The nanocomposites appear to contain Zn and Zn2+ ions, as indicated by their presence [54]. The core-level spectra of O 1 s show a wide asymmetric curve with two peaks with binding energies of 531.5 and 529.6 eV, demonstrating the presence of two oxygen species. Fig. 1 i ( Fig. S2c, S3c ). The peak at 531.5 eV corresponds to the normal oxygen signal in the hydroxyl group on the surface. The peak at 529.8 eV, on the other hand, corresponds to the lattice oxygen in ZnFe2O4 crystals and agrees exactly with the lattice oxygen in ZnFe2O4 crystals[21]. The deconvoluted C-1 s XPS spectra of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs are illustrated in Fig. 1 j ( Fig. S2d, S3d ). The distinctive peak energy of CC bonds featuring sp2 orbitals is 284.5 eV, while the peak energies of C-O-C and CO bonds are 285.4 and 288.3 eV, respectively. As a result, the XPS data demonstrate the presence of CQDs as well as their interaction with ZnFe2O4 [55]. Both Raman spectroscopy and XPS can be utilized to examine the characteristics of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs and their improved potential for photodegradation processes. Using these techniques, we can acquire a better knowledge of how CQDs can interact with ZnFe2O4 MSs in a solution, which will assist the design of photodegradation catalysts.

The structure and surface morphology of as-prepared ZnFe2O4 and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs were studied through scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 a and b show the SEM images of the annealed ZnFe2O4, which clearly show the sphere-like structure with micro size, after the modification of ZnFe2O4 with CQDs through the ultrasonication-calcination method, the ZnFe2O4 maintain its morphology, but we can see the CQDs nanoparticles on the surface of ZnFe2O4 which play a very important role in the photocatalytic activity of organic dye degradation. The broad surface morphological characteristics of CQDs and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs were studied using TEM HRTEM. Pure CQDs showed a homogeneous particle (sphere-shaped) morphology by diverse particle sizes (Fig. c, d). Fig. 2 e indicates the TEM image of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 to identify the attachment of CQDs on the surface of ZnFe2O4 material, whereas, Fig. 2 f and g show the high magnification HRTEM images of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. The fringes’ size of d = 0.254 nm was also observed, which agreed well with the (311) plane of the cubic ZnFe2O4 structure.

Furthermore, the corresponding SAED (inset Fig. 2 g) shows the polycrystalline nature of synthesized CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs; The STEM mode was used to analyze the elemental distributions of zinc, iron, oxygen, and carbon within the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. The uniform distribution of Zn, Fe, O, and carbon throughout the particular area is indicated in Fig. 2 h. As shown in ( Fig. S4 ), the energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) for the specified area of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites represents Zn, Fe, oxygen, and carbon energy peaks, which indicates that the CQDs were successfully loaded into ZnFe2O4 spheres. Moreover, CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs have a stoichiometric proportion of four elements, summed up in ( Table S3 ).

Nitrogen sorption studies were carried out to understand the pore structure and textural features of the prepared ZnFe2O4 and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. The analysis of typical N2 sorption isotherms reveals that both curves exhibit a type IV characteristic curves, accompanied by an H3 type hysteresis loop ( Fig. 3 a). This suggests that ZnFe2O4 and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs possess mesoporous properties, which the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BET) pore size distribution plots (Fig. 3 b). The average pore volume, BET specific surface area, and average pore size of pure ZnFe2O4 and CQDs-modified ZnFe2O4 are shown in Table S4, corresponding to 0.15, 0.30, and 0.45% by weight of the CQDs that changed ZnFe2O4. A significant difference has been observed between the pure and CQDs-modified ZnFe2O4; due to its larger surface area, narrow pore size, and bulky pore volume, the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs play an essential role in the organic pollutants degradation.

The optical absorption properties of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4 modified with CQDs were investigated using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), as illustrated in Fig. 3 d. The absorption properties of the as-synthesized materials were measured in the range of 200–800 nm as shown in Fig. 3 c. ZnFe2O4 displayed light absorption both in UV and visible region, the visible-light absorption efficiency of 0.30 CQDs/ ZnFe2O4 MSs with a bandgap of 1.78 eV was much greater than that of pure ZnFe2O4, perhaps due to the photosensitization capabilities of CQDs[56], representing the integration of CQDs with ZnFe2O4 might enhance the visible-light absorption efficiency. Based on the Tauc expression, the bandgap energy of the as-prepared compounds was calculated[57]. The bandgap (Eg) values for ZnFe2O4 0.15CQDs/ ZnFe2O4, 0.30 CQDs/ ZnFe2O4, and 0.45 CQDs/ ZnFe2O4 were determined using the intercept of the curve tangent to the abscissa axis ( Fig. 3 d). As a result, the bandgap (Eg) values for ZnFe2O4, 0.15CQDs/ ZnFe2O4, 0.30 CQDs/ ZnFe2O4, and 0.45 CQDs/ ZnFe2O4 MSs were determined to be 2.2, 2.0, 1.78, and 1.9 eV, which is compatible with the literature [58]. The photocatalytic activity of the catalyst can not only be enhanced by the band gap but different other factors can also influence it. The band gap is the only important factor in determining the energy needed to excite the electron from the conduction band to the valance band. Other factors are high surface area, surface defects, and the type of catalyst that affects the photocatalytic activity. In this work, carbon CQDs played an important role in the enhancement of the photocatalytic of the ZnFe2O4 since it reduces the band gap of the ZnFe2O4, and increases the surface area and surface defects of the catalyst.

MB and RhB organic dyes, commonly found in fabric wastes, are frequently used as a model pollutant in wastewater treatment to evaluate the catalysts' efficiency. The photocatalytic organic dye degradation was observed utilizing the specific absorption peaks of MB and RhB (= 664 nm for MB and =553 nm for RhB) as a function of irradiation time over CQDs/ZnFe2O4 (
Fig. 4 a and d). As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the λmax gradually shifted for MB dye as the absorbance decreased due to UV-light exposure; the λmax exhibited blue shifts due to the N-demethylation of MB dye. There is a visible broadening of absorption bands caused by Azure B (AB), Azure A (AA), and Azure C (AC), correspondingly. These absorption bands at 654–648 nm (irradiation time, 10 minutes), 620 nm (30 minutes), and 610 nm (60 minutes) are caused by Azure B (AB), Azure A (AA), and Azure C (AC), respectively [59] . The spectrum evolution of RhB ( Fig. 4 d) shows that the absorbance was depleted without the absorption peak shifting to the blue region. As reported by previous studies[60,61] in the bulk solution, ●OH radicals primarily attack the aromatic chromophore ring, resulting in the breakdown of the RhB structure and a decrease in absorbance without wavelength shift. Another possibility is that the aromatic rings are de-ethylated, resulting in massive blue wavelength changes. Our results show that the RhB is degraded via the previously described mechanism. Additionally, the digital image (the insets in Fig. 4 a, d) reveals the degradation of MB and RhB solutions by exhibiting colorlessness of the deep colors, which indicates that both organic dyes are almost completely degraded. According to the above analysis, the prepared composite of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 offers tremendous potential for wastewater treatment as an excellent photocatalyst. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation rates of MB and RhB over CQDs/ZnFe2O4 were examined under UV light irradiation. Fig. 4 b illustrates the temporal relationship between the relative concentration of MB (C/C0) and time, where C represents the concentration of MB at time t of irradiation and C0 denotes its initial concentration. Under similar conditions for experiments, the photocatalytic performance of ZnFe2O4 and 0.15 CQDs/ZnFe2O4, 0.3 CQDs/ZnFe2O4, and 0.45 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 are studied simultaneously. Detailed control studies on adsorption decolorization with photocatalysts in the dark and photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV irradiation with or without 0.3 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs are being conducted to explain the overall effects of adsorption, photolysis, and photocatalysis. Due to irradiation of UV light, the as-synthesized 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs adsorption properties are investigated. The photocatalysts adsorption-desorption equilibrium with MB is attained after 40 minutes in the dark, as shown in Fig. 4a. When equilibrium is attained, the 0.3CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs exhibit the greatest adsorption discoloration of 13.21%. The increased BET SSA and pore volume of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs may explain their enhanced adsorption capabilities. Notably, the blank tests under UV light (using only MB without any catalysts) expose low photolysis, with only a small amount of MB degrading under the irradiation of UV light, indicating the relative stability of MB under UV light irradiation. Though, when a photocatalyst is used in association with the irradiation of UV light, the degradation rates of MB are greatly increased, indicating that the MB degradation in this study is mostly due to a photocatalytic procedure. Additionally, the experiments demonstrate that the as-prepared 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs exhibit the strongest photocatalytic activity, with photocatalytic degradation percentages of MB reaching almost 100% directly after 60 minutes of the UV light irradiation, significantly higher than those of ZnFe2O4 and other composites. Below UV light irradiation, Fig. 4 e illustrates the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB catalyzed by ZnFe2O4, 0.15 CQDs/ZnFe2O4, 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4, and 0.45 CQDs/ZnFe2O4. Without photocatalysts, direct Photodegradation of RhB was negligible when exposed to UV light, confirming RhB strong stability under irradiation of UV light. Before UV light irradiation, an adsorption/desorption equilibrium is reached b/w the photocatalyst and RhB during photocatalytic studies. It is also worth noting that the 0.3CQDs/ZnFe2O4 has a higher adsorption capacity for RhB when compared to single-phase ZnFe2O4 and other composites, with the composite exhibiting up to 10.12% adsorption discoloration. With the assistance of ZnFe2O4 and 100 minutes of UV light irradiation, approximately 56.35% of RhB is degraded, yielding less than 0.45 CQDs/ZnFe2O4. Most interestingly, the highest photocatalytic activity of 0.30 CQDsZnFe2O4 was determined 95.55% for photocatalytic degradation of RhB under the same conditions, demonstrating the synergistic effect of CQDs on ZnFe2O4 effectively accelerates RhB degradation. The data above demonstrate clearly that 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 has better photocatalytic activity to degrade both MB and RhB organic dyes when exposed to UV light. Additionally, as illustrated in ( table S6 ), the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs is greater than previously reported in the literature. As illustrated in Fig. 4 c and f, the photodegradation of MB and RhB adhere to a pseudo-first-order rate equation, i.e., ln(C0/C0) = −Kt. The 0.30CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs sample displayed good photocatalytic activity with K values for MB and RhB dyes below UV light described in ( table S5 ). This is significantly greater than ZnFe2O4 and other composites. The enhanced considerable result obtained with the 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs combination demonstrates the critical significance of CQDs. On the other hand, photocatalytic properties of photocatalysts depend on various parameters, including particle size and surface area (BET). We analyzed the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnFe2O4 samples with and without CQDs to understand further and illustrate the charge carrier migration and separation process. It was worthwhile to investigate whether a CQD coating exhibited emission properties. In addition, the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. As shown in Fig. S5, the optimal emission wavelength for all samples was approximately 465 nm. According to Fig. S5, ZnFe2O4 generated the highest fluorescence intensity. The low intensity of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs provided evidence that ZnFe2O4 was affected by CQDs. Additionally, the 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 nanocomposite displayed the minimum photoluminescence intensity, representing an increase in electron-hole pair separation capacity and demonstrating the optimal photocatalytic capacity than pure ZnFe2O4 and other CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composites [62,63]. On the one hand, the CQDs enhanced photocatalysts' ability to separate electrons and holes, increasing their photocatalytic performance [64]. On the other hand, CQDs had a greater impact on delaying the photoluminescent recombination of electrons and holes [65]. Finally, doping with CQDs improved the spectrum characteristics of the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 composite, decreasing the hybrid nanomaterials photoluminescence intensity.

Based on the given experimental findings and subsequent characterization studies, a possible photodegradation mechanism for organic dyes over 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs is presented in Fig. 5 b
. The charge-transfer process can be explained by calculating the photocatalytic catalysts conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) [66,67].

ECB and EVB represent the conduction and valence band edge potentials, respectively. Ee represents the standard hydrogen electrode (4.5 eV vs. NHE), while Eg represents the free electron energy of a semiconductor. The letter "X" denoted the geometric mean of the constituent elements' absolute electronegativity [68]. Based on previous studies, the minimum and maximum conduction values of bare ZnFe2O4 in the conduction band were −1.02 and 0.76 eV above Ef correspondingly [69]. Consequently, excited electrons on the CB of ZnFe2O4 reduced O2 to ●O2 −, While the holes in the VB were unable to oxidize OH− to produce ●OH, as E
0 (O2/●O2 −) was around 0.046 eV and E
0 (OH/OH−) was 2.38 eV vs. NHE [48,70]. We conducted active species trapping studies to elucidate the photocatalytic process of 0.3 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs. As illustrated in Fig. 4 g, h, when AgNO3 (a scavenger of photogenerated electron (e−) is added, the organic dye degradation percentage somewhat increases compared to when no scavenger is added. The primary reason for this is the efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs facilitated by the photoinduced electron consumption of AgNO3. When ethanol (a radical scavenger) is added, the proportion of dye degraded increases, showing that ●OH is also an active species in this photocatalytic activity. When ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA, a scavenger of photogenerated holes (h+), is added, the degradation efficiency of the dye increases as well, showing that h+ plays a part in this photocatalysis as well. Fig. 5 b illustrates the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/ZnFe2O4 hybrid materials based on the aforementioned experimental results. When UV light irradiates ZnFe2O4, electrons can be excited from the VB to the CB, leaving the VB with holes. Usually, these photogenerated electrons and holes recombine rapidly, with just a small fraction of charges participating in the photocatalytic activity. When ZnFe2O4 is combined with CQDs to make hybrid composites, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of ZnFe2O4 tend to move to the CQDs. CQDs delocalized conjugated structure facilitated electron transfer, resulting in effective electron-hole pair separation and increased photocatalytic performance. ●O2 − radicals are thought to play a significant role in photocatalysis. Additionally, hydroxyl radicals were generated in this system and attributed to photocatalytic degradation. As illustrated in Fig. 4 i, a good photocatalyst has high photocatalytic activity and high photocatalytic stability. Recycling tests were conducted to assess the stability of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 during photocatalytic activity. Four sequential cycles of 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs were considered. After four cycles, the 0.30 CQDs/ZnFe 2 O 4 MSs can still demonstrate high photocatalytic activity. Additionally, As shown in Fig. S6 , the XRD peaks of CQDsZnFe2O4 after four cycles retained all CQDs/ZnFe2O4 distinctive peaks. This analysis shows that the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs are not only photo-catalytically active but also stable over time and exhibit no obvious photo erosion during the photocatalytic decomposition of MB molecules is important for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic structure of the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 heterostructure interface
ce:section.ce:para: To get insights into the electronic features of the prepared composite, we systematically conducted the DFT calculations using the Quantum Espresso package. First of all, we optimized the structure of ZnFe2O4, CQDs, and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 heterostructures. The top and side views of optimized most stable geometrical structures of ZnFe2O4, CQDs, and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 heterostructures are shown in Fig. 6a, e, and I (Fig. S7, S8, S9) respectively. To get information about the surface charge affinity of constructed models of ZnFe2O4, CQDs, and CQDs/ZnFe2O4, the charge distribution profile was calculated as shown in Fig. 6 b, f, and j, indicating high charge distribution over the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 heterostructure. Further, to get insight into the electronic properties of the constructed models, we computed their density of states (DOS), projected density of states (PDOS), and band structure. Fig. 6 c, g, and k showed the PDOS of ZnFe2O4, CQDs, and CQDs/ZnFe2O4, indicating a large density of states and high-density distribution, contributed by each atomic orbital due to hybridization over the fermi level, indicating higher electronic conductivity under the charge carrier migration way for the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 heterostructure as compared to ZnFe2O4 and CQDs. The C 2p state contribution to the valence band (VB) was evident in the PDOS of CQDs, whereas the O 2p state contribution was to the conduction band (CB). The Fermi level analysis revealed a notable hybridization occurring between the O and Cp states, as evidenced by the abundance of band populations observed in the band structure of CQDs. A comparable phenomenon was also noted in the lower CB. The PDOS of the ZnFe2O4 surface indicated the contribution of each O(2p) and Zn(d) state in the VB close to the Fermi level due to remarkable hybridization between both orbitals in the VB. In addition, Fe(d) and O(p) states mainly contributed to the lesser CB and upper CB, respectively. Therefore, incorporating ZnFe2O4 and CQDs significantly raised the energy states over the fermi level, as also supported by the band structure of the CQDs/ZnFe2O4 Fig. 6 d, h, l. According to the DFT calculations, CQDs/ZnFe2O4 will contain novel energy states within the level of Fermi, which will not just reduce the optical band gap but additionally provide acceptor states (trap sites) over photogenerated electrons, thereby enhancing the process of charge separation. The aforementioned attributes will improve the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs adorned ZnFe2O4 MS.

In summary, CQD-modified ZnFe2O4 MSs photocatalysts were synthesized via a direct impregnation-thermal technique. After being treated with CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of the hybrid materials in the degradation of MB and RhB organic dyes increased significantly when exposed to UV light. The considerable enhancement in photocatalytic activity (∼100% degradation of MB in 60 min and ∼95% degradation of RhB in 80 min) was ascribed to the critical role of CQDs in the ZnFe2O4 samples treated with CQDs. The first principle calculations validated the importance of CQDs and the ZnFe2O4 interface. For example, the novel energy states generated by the C 2p of the CQDs structure demonstrated the origin of improved electron-hole separation when compared with pure ZnFe2O4. The computational study also recognized the reciprocation of CQDs with the surface of ZnFe2O4, which may alter their photodegradation reaction. A proposed degradation mechanism for the CQDs-modified ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst was presented based on experimental and theoretical results. Apart from the photocatalytic degradation activity, our work can greatly motivate the designing and synthesis strategies of other CQD-modified semiconducting materials.
